channel_title: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
title: Product Building Strategy Part 1: How to build product for Dishwasher Problems
executive_summary: In the video 'Product Building Strategy Part 1: How to build product for Dishwasher Problems', Nate B Jones discusses the concept of the 'dishwasher problem', where solutions provide invisible value that often goes unrecognized by customers. He emphasizes the need to focus on core functionality and build complete solutions in mature markets, where customer expectations are already set. The video highlights the importance of creating functional value, even if it lacks glamour, to meet consumer needs effectively.
bullets: The term 'dishwasher problem' refers to solving a problem that results in 'invisible value' that you generally don't get credit for. This means that even when you fix an issue, customers may not recognize the effort or benefit, similar to how a dishwasher cleans dishes without anyone noticing the work it does.

Focusing on core functionality is crucial; products should be reliable, low friction, and easy to use. If a product is like a car that constantly breaks down, no one will appreciate its features, just like a complicated tool that is hard to operate will frustrate users.

Building for the full product gap means providing a complete solution to a problem, rather than just addressing part of it. Think of it like fixing a leaky roof; if you only patch one hole, the rain will still come in through others, leaving the problem unsolved.

In mature markets, customers have clear expectations, leaving little room for surprising and delighting them. It's like going to a well-known restaurant; you expect the same great meal every time, and if it’s not delivered, you’ll be disappointed.

Building with invisible value requires recognizing the lack of glamour in the space and being willing to create meaningful value. This is akin to a janitor who keeps a building clean; their work is essential but often goes unnoticed until something goes wrong.

Functional value can be appreciated even if it is not flashy or fancy. Imagine a sturdy pair of shoes; they may not be trendy, but if they last for years and keep your feet comfortable, their true value shines through over time.

To truly address the 'dishwasher problem', you must understand that customers may not appreciate the invisible value you provide. Like a good friend who always helps you move but never asks for recognition, your efforts might go unnoticed but are still essential.

You can either repair to reach a previous state, build for parity with competitors, or create a non-remarkable solution. This is like choosing between fixing an old bike, making a new one just like everyone else’s, or inventing a bike that doesn’t stand out but gets you from point A to B.

If there's not a lot of excitement in the space, and you're solving a functional problem, don't expect to generate excitement from it. It's like trying to throw a surprise party for someone who hates surprises; you might be wasting your effort.

The speaker emphasizes that 'if there's not a ton of excitement in the space and you are building to solve a functional problem that consumers need solved, you're not going to generate excitement from it.' This means that sometimes, the best solutions are the most straightforward ones.

Recognizing that functional value often lacks glamour is key to building effective products. Just like a reliable old pickup truck may not be flashy but gets the job done, functional products can be the backbone of customer satisfaction.

Building products that solve functional problems requires a commitment to understanding customer needs deeply. Think of it like a doctor diagnosing an illness; without thorough examination and understanding, the treatment may not address the real issue.
key_takeaways: DO focus on core functionality in your product design. This matters because a reliable and easy-to-use product will meet customer expectations and encourage repeat business. If you skip this, you risk creating a product that frustrates users and fails to solve their problems.

DO aim to build for the full product gap. Providing a complete solution ensures that customers feel satisfied and valued. If you only address part of their needs, they may seek alternatives that offer a more comprehensive solution.

DO recognize the importance of invisible value in your products. Understanding that not all value is immediately visible can help you create solutions that genuinely improve customer experiences. If you overlook this, you may underestimate the impact of your work.

technical_terms.dishwasher problem: This term describes a situation where the value provided by a product or service is not recognized by the customer. Think of it like a dishwasher that cleans dishes efficiently, but no one appreciates the effort until it breaks down.
technical_terms.core functionality: This refers to the essential features of a product that ensure it works effectively and meets user needs. Like the engine in a car, if the core functionality fails, the entire product becomes useless.
technical_terms.full product gap: This is the complete range of solutions needed to address a customer's problem. Imagine a puzzle; if you only provide half the pieces, the picture will never be complete.
technical_terms.expected value: This is the level of benefit that customers anticipate from a product based on their previous experiences. Like going to a favorite restaurant, you expect the same quality meal each time.
technical_terms.invisible value: This is the benefit provided by a product that customers may not consciously recognize or appreciate. Think of it like the air conditioning in a car; you enjoy the comfort it provides but rarely think about how it works.
technical_terms.functional value: This is the practical benefit that a product offers, regardless of its aesthetic appeal. Like a sturdy pair of work boots, they may not be stylish, but their durability and comfort are what truly matter.
technical_terms.mature markets: These are established markets where customer expectations are well-defined and competition is fierce. Like a crowded marketplace, everyone knows what to expect, and standing out requires exceptional effort.
technical_terms.repairing to reach a previous state: This means fixing something to restore it to how it was before. It's like mending a torn shirt; you're not improving it, just bringing it back to its original condition.
technical_terms.building for parity: This refers to creating a product that is on par with competitors, without offering anything new or innovative. Like making a generic cola that tastes just like the leading brand; it may satisfy thirst but lacks uniqueness.
technical_terms.non-remarkable solution space: This is a category where products do not stand out and offer similar features. Think of it like a sea of beige paint; they all look the same and don't excite anyone.
